speaker: interviewer
text: You ready to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, ma'am.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2, Phyllis Dreazen speaking with Allen Caine. It's October 23, 1995. We're in Philadelphia. How did things change when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lost our friends. Gentile friends, we lost them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were a few farmers. The, the war broke out in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] September.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 1st.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly what date. And the stuff was still in the field-- the farmers. So I happened to know a big farmer, very rich. I went out to him. I help him take off the beets, potatoes. I was a good worker. They fed me good. They knew my parents, my grandparents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked there for a, a long while. When I wanted to go home, he says to me, time-- are changed. Please, he said, I'll hide you. Stay with us. Nobody would know that you're here. You'll live through the war. But one God knows what's going to happen to you Jews. [SIGHS] I said, no, I cannot do that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was a sick man. My grandparents, I promised them I'll help them. I better go home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first-- the first few weeks they came in, they gave us an order right away to wear patches, not the Star of David, just plain round patches-- yellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you the order?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German government. There was still a military government. They gave the order-- one on the right side-- front right, and one on the left shoulder blade. I never wore. This way, I could go into a Gentile place, buy some meat, buy some bread. They would--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were blond as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very-- white hair. And I spoke fluently. As a matter of fact, a lot of times I had to interpret between Polish-- Polish and German, I had to interpret, which I did. And that was a little easier for us. Then I used to go out on the-- on the farms, which you weren't allowed-- a Jew was not allowed to be on the farm. Everybody had to be in the city. But they never knew who I am. I used to bring home potatoes. I didn't pay for them. I knew a few farmers. I never paid for it. They used to give me bread. They used to bake bread. Farmers-- the farmers in Poland always had enough to eat. And that was going-- it was going on for a while. And later on, I was just plain-- I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there selections going on or deportations?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] First when they came in, they took out intelligentsia-- former mayor, former police chief, and the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the big, big shots-- took them to the woods, shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Of course, with them, they used to take a couple Jews with it. So they-- the Nazis were afraid for an uprise. For the ordinary Polish fellow, his, his brains are not as good to be-- weren't as good to make an uprise. They had to have a, a, a, a 'Fuhrer,' what they call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: A leader. So that's what they did. That's like cutting the head off from somebody. And later on, they, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they made a committee, a Jewish committee, and a head man over the committee, all the people. And every day, somebody has to go. In other words, once a week, every Jew had to put a day's work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I sold myself out. If a guy didn't want to go to work, he paid me. I took his place. And that's how I made a couple bucks. When you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to work, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything, wherever there were-- hardest work. We used to-- electric poles, we used to carry. Sit on the highway, cut stones, little stones, about-- [POINTS] crush them. And they made a highway out of that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sweep the streets. Clean the toilets in the-- in the, the Nazi's houses, anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much would you get for a day like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: For that, we never got anything, never got anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if someone would pay you to go in his place, what would you get for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like $2 a day. Like $2 a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the members of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's the right word, 'Judenrat.' That's what they-- that's in German, 'Judenrat.' They were nice people. They were, you know, big men from before the war. They were educated people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one, the head of the-- head of the whole thing, his name was Tienits. As a matter of fact, he never came to Auschwitz. We had a castle in our hometown. I took pictures of it. That, that castle is a thousand years old. And they-- I came with the first shipment to Auschwitz. The second shipment, they took every-- the Jews into that castle. And that Tienits, that head of the Jews, his son had to hang his father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just-- and before he hanged him, his father says, my son, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Amalek. Amalek was a nation once. When the Jews came out of Egypt, they were attacked by that nation. Their name was Amalek. And it's a-- it's a verse in the Torah, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Amalek. But [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Germans. Don't forget what the bad problem was.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't live long, the son. He got killed, too. In the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same place, after he hung his father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ordered it, Germans or Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, the Germans did, the German police, their state police, Stadtpolizei.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stadtpolizei.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You alluded to the fact that there were Nazis living in your town, that you cleaned their houses. How many? And who were they, if you remember any?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You mean at-- you're already in the war. There were-- Gestapo lived in our town. The headquarters from the Gestapo was in our town. They made our town-- it's like a-- it was a nice-sized city in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like 35,000 in our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: A quarter Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 8,000 Jews lived in the city. But all the Jews lived downtown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the houses belonged to them, which-- the, the Germans gave an order to tear them down. So we didn't have a ghetto. There was no room for a ghetto. So we were the first ones to get into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the Germans live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away from the intelligentsia-- they took away. Beautiful buildings they took away. And that's where they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the leader from the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, his name was Roseman.

speaker: interviewer
text: First name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I don't know the first name. I'll tell you. See, I was apprentice boy. They figured, as a little boy, about-- when the war broke out, I was about 14 years, just finished-- not even quite 14 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I started my trade, they used to send me. And they, they thought that I'll be respected by them. So I used to work for that guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're making closets in Europe that you can take it apart, put it together, take apart-- and not closet in the walls. It's closet-- put itself. And I worked for that guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came over to help me. So my hand was shaking. He says to me, [GERMAN]-- why all the Jews are afraid of me? You know, what am I going to tell him? Because he's a murderer. So one, one day came an order for the Gestapo to move-- to move out to a, a different city. So I worked for them. And I packed their furniture in crates. And I packed a mirror. By the time the mirror went there, it was broken up. I didn't have the right equipment for it, the right boards, whatever. They came back. One guy came back. He was looking for me. And so whoever saw him-- it was a family, a rich family. Geldert was their name. How do I know that? Because that same man lives in, in Mexico City. So when he asked them where so-and-so is, he-- he'd asked him, what did he do, do wrong? So the Gestapo told him, he broke the mirror. Not that I didn't-- that I packed it wrong, but he broke the mirror. They bought him another mirror.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they packed it. And everything was all right. They wouldn't tell me what it was. So I didn't even know. After the war, I found out. If they would find me, I would have got shot right on the spot. And that man, like I mentioned before, he lives in Mexico City. He mentioned it to me. We talked about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But to them, didn't matter how old you are, who you are. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he one of the bad, bad, guys, Roseman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Yes, it was him. It was Roseman. There was a Lifka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's, it's hard to remember them. There was a Gutsman, 'Goetz-mann,' Gutsman. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, one of the Gutsman, when I brought my uncle-- when we brought our uncle from Warsaw Ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we gave him 100 mark. He gave him a an Ausweis-- [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] document. That's how we bribed him. So my uncle would have a document.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the Nazis left your town. And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the Nazis, just the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis were still there. Thing was that they were the head of the Nazis. We had the Yellow Shirts, the Brown Shirts, the Black Shirts. We had them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very colorful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very colorful, yes. And also, we weren't allowed to go on the-- walk on the pavement. If a person, like-- you saw a car, they would run you over purposely. You put a foot on the-- on the pavement, you would pay a fine. If you would jump up on the pavement, either he then would kill him or he would go to Auschwitz. We had a few of them. They-- I met them later in Auschwitz, just for that purpose. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a Nazi could come over if he, he saw a Jew, kill him on the spot, and just walk away. Nobody would do anything to them-- to him. We were just like outlaw people.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] And frightened all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a curfew from 1939. Till we came to Birkenau, we had a curfew, 6 o'clock. I had a Ausweis. I had papers that I'm allowed because we used to work sometimes 24 hours a day. So I was allowed with it. But still and all, they catch you at night walking, they beat you up, with the papers, with everything, no matter what you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They were drunk. Then after I told them I'm working for the Burgermeister, he relaxed a little bit. And he, he went back. And he let me live my-- let me alone. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had Polish guys, too. They-- what they became-- they joined the party. They used to pick on us, too. But with them, it wasn't so bad because most of them couldn't speak too good German. But the only thing that bothers us, they became Nazis, our neighbors, our friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they become really Nazis or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they became--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to try and get by?

speaker: interviewee
text: --really Nazis. And they never believed it. When they had to go to the army, then they, they there was sorry they ever did it. But it was too late. The first thing they did is push them to the Russian front. 1% came back. Most of them died, get-- got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] So as you saw these deplorable conditions, what did you think was happening or was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We knew what was going to happen. We-- one-- one day, they closed up the whole town, took out the few thousand Jewish people, put them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out about 20 kilometers. They made a ghetto down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neustadt, Neustadt. New, 'New City,' that's what that word mean. And down there, an epidemic of typhus broke out. They were dying like flies. I have friends here. They were in that city. Her parents died. It was very bad-- dirty conditions. They got them with a fence-- fenced up, no food, no nothing, whatever they could bring from outside. They made up a Jewish police. Some of them were all right. And some of them were collaborators.

speaker: interviewer
text: Between 1939 and 1942, what else happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] One time, they came looking for somebody to go to work. All the Jews should come out. We had a open space like a market. Every-- twice a week, the farmers used to bring stuff and sell it in that place. Now, they made a little forest-- the middle. Now it's a park. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After all the Jews came out, they went to look for some more to make sure that nobody's hiding. They found six people hiding. They took them out. They put them in jail. And they were waiting for an order from Eichmann what to do with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a week later, an order came to hang him. So we worked for the government. So they came into our shop. And we made the setup. They gave us the blueprints and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every able body, every person-- if you had an infant, you had to leave the infant by itself-- you had to come to that marketplace and watch the hanging. When we got there, there was Gestapo with machine gun, the heavy machine guns, facing us. When they were-- they called out so-and-so, these-- the head of the Jewish people, there was another one also. His name was Eric. He said, those six people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Gestapo gave him a warning. Don't ever say 'those people' because you guys are not people. You are not even dogs because we like dogs. You're just Juden.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave him a warning for the next time. And they hung those six people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they dropped them. And now, there's buildings built on top of them. They lay buried in that place. Nobody took them out. During those years between '39 and '42, what else did you experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special. People committed suicide. People saw that we're not going to live through. Then we heard that this country went into war. We was hoping that they-- they'll come, help the, the Russians. Then he attacked the Russians. A lot of-- more people went to the Russian side because Russia took over half of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But what happened, the Russians wouldn't let in-- let the people in. So they got stuck in no-man's-land. They froze to death. Some of them froze their feet. They had to be amputated. Their hands had to be amputated. Hundreds and hundreds were laying down there on the ground and waiting to-- so the German-- the-- there was no return back. So finally, then Hitler gave a-- not-- Stalin gave an order to open the gates and let the-- let those Jews in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were working in the job carpentry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening with the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working. And my oldest brother was working. And my-- Zelek, the second brother, was a tailor, a good tailor. He worked for the Nazis. He did get paid, but half the amount as a Pole-- as a Christian would get, half the amount. But he made money. And my brother Chaim, the, the one before me, he worked for the governor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kind-- all kind of work, hard work. But he never get-- he didn't get paid. And I used to get paid 25 pennies an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was already dead. He was killed in '41, erev Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him in after they take-- they chased the intelligentsia out. So they went-- they had to go in and-- through the crosses and statues, everything. He wasn't fast enough because he was a religious man. He respected his religion and somebody else's also. And they kept hollering, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he wasn't fast enough. They beat him up. Two days later, he died. He said, I have nothing to live for. And that was the end of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents, they stayed. They came together. I was trying to help them out. I worked. I used to bring home to my grandparents pieces of wood because-- for cooking and everything. And sometimes my grandfather would want a-- get a haircut. I would help him out. That's all I could help him out because I was hungry myself. And I didn't have anything for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father died, was it still possible to have a proper burial for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We even got a death Schein-- a Totenschein. That means a death certificate. [SIGHS] Four of us buried my father. I could hardly reach the-- because we don't have boxes in Europe. You don't bury a person in, in a box. They bury them open grave, posts around it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And today, there is nothing left. The three-- three-story-high houses are built on those places. The stones are gone. We had [NON-ENGLISH]-- an [NON-ENGLISH]. That's where a tzadik gets buried. They're little buildings above it. These are gone, nothing left. What they did with him, I don't know. There's no more stones left. We don't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get your food by rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but we got the ration taken-- things. And we could-- we couldn't do anything with them. We couldn't get nothing with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't any. There wasn't any. If there was any, they gave it to the Polish population. And they didn't have too many, too much. They had better than we had, but not too much, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in an ordinary day, what would you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes, just potatoes and water. Just-- you know, we cooked them. We would never throw the water out. We just mashed them right in the water like a pulp. And that's how we ate. And that was our nourishment. You're lucky if you had a potato. Sometimes, I'll organize it, which-- 'organize it' means steal it. And probably you heard of that word. Organize it from someplace, a few potatoes, a little bit of coal, so we can have something to cook with.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were the 20,000 who were brought to Neustadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not 20,000, no, a couple thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: A couple thousand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were the intellectuals who were taken off. Who else was deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know how they made up a list that time. But I know one thing. My, my uncle, he-- the one we brought in from Warsaw, he was on that list, too. And we carried on. We had influenced. I'm, I'm-- I was a funny guy. I could have gone straight even to Hitler and asked something like that, too. I wasn't afraid of nobody. And somehow, we got him. We-- they canceled him. He didn't go to Neustadt. He stayed with us. Unfortunately, you know what happened. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was him and his wife. Because it cost money and everything to bring him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you bring him to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was in Warsaw, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Warsaw was unbelievable. It was unbelievable. They used to, somehow-- they used to have a song. See, they-- down there, they got some kind of cards, whatever, a little slice of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to have a song. 'Make it faster.' So he-- 'and tell me where you left your card.' So that some of them were standing and waiting for the guy to die and to get the card from him-- Could--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for the piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sing us that song?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, [LAUGHS] no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The tune?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I don't know. My uncle used to tell me that. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now it's 1942. And now what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we started to hear rumors. They're going to send out people. They're going to send out people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we already-- we had our foreman, [? Gilbert, ?] Moishe [? Gilbert ?] was his name. And he had a-- he spoke with the architect, the one-- he gives us the drawings for whatever we had to do, that he should say a good word, that we should go-- that we can-- would go with the first shipment.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because the-- what, what they-- even the guy said, you guys going to get [NON-ENGLISH], resettled. Never mentioned anything about any camp or anything, just resettled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard anything about any camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. So they made-- every group had a captain. And one day, the, the, the 5th of-- the 5th of November, 1942, an order came the next day. Whatever you can carry on your-- whatever you can carry, come out in the morning. And that's-- that was that. There was around 10,000 the first-- the first shipment, was-- not 10,000, 1,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you come out to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The marketplace. Everything happened on the marketplace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people came out. They had a dozen shirts to put on, a dozen-- oh, the whole dozen on him. Well, I had nothing to worry about. I-- as I only had a couple shirts, not even that, one shirt. I didn't have much. But people brought with them the blankets and feather beds, everything. And we went out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of town. And we stayed in a warehouse overnight. And the next day, from over there, we marched by ourselves-- no police, no nothing. We-- next day, we marched to the train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not two miles away from the city. And we got there. And they gave us a passenger train, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just to make it look good, that we're not going to any bad places, that we are going-- really getting resettled.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning, they told us nicely to go in the-- inside. But soon as everybody was in, you could hear the shots because people opened up the, the windows and stuck their heads out. That was against the law. Nothing-- locked it, sealed it. And that was it. You're in it.
